questions.question: What specific engineering challenge does GDPR's 'Right to Erasure' (Article 17) create for Data Engineers working with immutable data stores?
options: It requires re-indexing all database tables monthly.

It requires physically deleting a specific user's data from all systems including immutable stores like historical Kafka topics and Parquet files in S3 — which don't support simple row-level deletion — forcing Data Engineers to design deletion-safe architectures from the start.
It requires all data to be encrypted with RSA-4096 algorithms.
It requires storing all data within the European Union only.
questions.answer: It requires physically deleting a specific user's data from all systems including immutable stores like historical Kafka topics and Parquet files in S3 — which don't support simple row-level deletion — forcing Data Engineers to design deletion-safe architectures from the start.
questions.explanation: Deleting from a transactional DB is a single SQL DELETE. Deleting from a Kafka topic with billions of events, or from 10TB of Parquet files, requires architectural solutions like Cryptographic Erasure.

questions.question: How does 'Cryptographic Erasure' technically satisfy GDPR deletion requirements for immutable data stores like Kafka?
options: It physically removes the message from the Kafka partition log.

Instead of storing raw PII, it stores encrypted PII. Each user's data is encrypted with their own unique key. To 'erase' a user, you simply delete their encryption key. The encrypted blobs become permanently unreadable without needing to touch the immutable data store.
It overwrites the Kafka partition with random bytes.
It converts the PII to an anonymized UUID in S3.
questions.answer: Instead of storing raw PII, it stores encrypted PII. Each user's data is encrypted with their own unique key. To 'erase' a user, you simply delete their encryption key. The encrypted blobs become permanently unreadable without needing to touch the immutable data store.
questions.explanation: Without the key, ciphertext is theoretically useless garbage. Deleting the key is legally equivalent to deleting the data itself under GDPR.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'Dynamic Data Masking' applied at the Data Warehouse column level?
options: It compresses sensitive column data automatically.

It enforces role-based access control at the data level. Different user roles see different versions of the same column — a Data Analyst might see a masked 'XXXX-1234' credit card number, while a privileged Data Engineer with the right role sees the full, unmasked number.
It converts column data types from VARCHAR to INTEGER.
It encrypts column data using AES-256 before disk storage.
questions.answer: It enforces role-based access control at the data level. Different user roles see different versions of the same column — a Data Analyst might see a masked 'XXXX-1234' credit card number, while a privileged Data Engineer with the right role sees the full, unmasked number.
questions.explanation: Masking is applied transparently at query time. The underlying data is unchanged — only what specific roles are allowed to see changes.
